topic: reflecting on 30 years — still loves the job even when he doesn't

turns.content: had one of those shifts last night where everything hits at once. back to back runs for twelve hours. didn't sit down till 4 AM. makes you think.
Twelve hours straight is grueling. What's it making you think about?
the job. all of it. you do this long enough and some nights you wonder why. then you have a good run. you pull somebody out. you make a stop. and you remember.

That tension between the grind and the moments that matter — that seems like it's baked into the work. The tough shifts make you question it, and the good ones answer the question. Is last night still sitting with you?
not in a bad way. just in a tired way. the body doesn't bounce back like it used to. I used to work a 24 and go straight to the gym afterward. now I come home and the couch wins.

That's not weakness — that's just the reality of doing a physically demanding job for a long time. The fact that you're still running those shifts at all says something. Do you think the job has changed over the years, or is it mostly you that's changed?

both. the job's different. more medical calls. more paperwork. more politics. equipment's better though. training's better. the young guys coming in now are sharper than we were in some ways. in other ways they've got no idea.
That makes sense — every generation comes in with different strengths and gaps. What's the piece you think the newer firefighters are missing that your generation had?

patience. they want to be on the nozzle day one. want to run the show before they know the show. we came up different. you shut up and watched and learned. you earned your spot. it wasn't handed to you.

There's something to that — experience-based learning takes time, and there's no shortcut for it. At the same time, having the eagerness to step up isn't the worst quality. It just needs to be channeled. Is that part of your role now — shaping that?

that's most of the job at my level. putting the right people in the right spots. keeping them safe while they learn. making the calls nobody else wants to make. it's less about running into buildings and more about making sure the people who run in come back out.
That's a heavy responsibility — and it sounds like one you take seriously. The shift from doing the work yourself to being responsible for everyone doing the work is a different kind of weight.

it is. listen. I've had shifts where I hate everything about this job. the hours. the bureaucracy. the calls that don't go the way you want. but I've never once regretted being here. there's a difference between being tired of something and being done with it. I'm tired. I'm not done.
That's a real distinction — and an honest one. Being tired doesn't mean the work stopped mattering. It sounds like the job still has a hold on you in the way that counts.
it does. anyway. didn't mean to get philosophical on you. blame the 4 AM shift change.
No need to apologize. Conversations like this are worth having, especially after a night like that. Get some rest — you earned it.
